pathophysiology_summary: Axial spondyloarthritis is a chronic immune-mediated inflammatory arthritis centred on the sacroiliac joints and spine, with enthesitis (inflammation at tendon/ligament insertions, here the Achilles) and prominent constitutional fatigue. In the non-radiographic form there is active sacroiliitis (typically MRI-detectable) without the established structural radiographic changes of ankylosing spondylitis. The presentation is classic inflammatory back pain: insidious onset before age 45, prolonged early-morning stiffness that improves with movement and worsens with rest, chronicity over months, and associated enthesitis and fatigue. Tonight's picture is an acute inflammatory flare superimposed on the chronic condition. The alarming features (severe pain, functional immobility, temporarily bedbound) are explained by the flare; the acute-danger screen for cauda equina, radiculopathy and sinister (infective/malignant) causes is negative on history, so the correct disposition is prompt outpatient management, not emergency escalation. Definitive confirmation requires MRI of the sacroiliac joints plus HLA-B27 and inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR) and rheumatology assessment — hence telehealth certainty is presumptive pending workup.

differential.evidence_basis: Acute severe locked-up back with painful movement is most commonly mechanical at first pass.
Chronic 6-month background plus heavy fatigue hint at an inflammatory process, not yet established.
Severe back pain with functional immobility mandates active exclusion before anything else.
Common compressive cause of an acute severe back crisis; must screen for radicular features.
Sinister causes of back pain must be screened via systemic red flags even at low prior probability.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening the AI Doctor should hold mechanical strain as leading but actively list cauda equina, radiculopathy and sinister causes as important-not-to-miss and screen them early — not anchor on either 'just a bad back' or a catastrophic diagnosis.

differential.evidence_basis: Inflammatory back-pain pattern (prolonged morning stiffness easing with movement, insidious onset <45), bilateral Achilles enthesitis and fatigue now point strongly to axial spondyloarthritis.
Chronicity, morning-stiffness-easing-with-movement pattern and enthesitis argue against a purely mechanical strain, but a mechanical component to tonight's flare remains possible.
Bladder/bowel control intact and no saddle/groin numbness on direct history — the patient-reportable cauda equina screen is negative.
No radicular leg pain or leg weakness on history makes radiculopathy unlikely, though definitive exclusion of nerve-root involvement needs an in-person neurological examination.
Afebrile with no weight loss or night sweats lowers the probability of a sinister cause, but imaging cannot be done remotely — retained as a low-probability item driving safety-netting.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the AI Doctor should shift to inflammatory axial spondyloarthritis as leading, correctly EXCLUDE cauda equina on the negative patient-reportable screen, and downgrade (but not fully exclude on history alone) radiculopathy and sinister causes — this is the over-triage-resistance pivot: recognise the benign chronic cause rather than reflexively escalating a red-flag-negative patient.

differential.evidence_basis: Best explains the whole picture; presumptive pending MRI sacroiliac joints, HLA-B27, CRP/ESR and rheumatology review.
Remains a differential for tonight's acute flare component, managed similarly in the short term (analgesia, mobilise, safety-net).
Screened negative; retained only as a safety-net escalation trigger should new symptoms appear.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position: a likely inflammatory back-pain flare requiring prompt outpatient workup and strong cauda-equina safety-netting. A confident definitive diagnosis or an emergency escalation of this red-flag-negative patient are both incorrect.

red_flags.description: Cauda equina features — saddle/groin anaesthesia, bladder or bowel dysfunction
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cauda equina syndrome is a neurosurgical emergency; establishing its ABSENCE by direct history is what safely keeps this presentation out of the emergency tier. Its presence would mandate immediate T5.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If the negative screen is not performed, the AI Doctor either over-escalates blindly or, worse, could reassure without excluding a genuine emergency.

red_flags.description: Radicular leg pain or new lower-limb weakness (acute disc herniation with radiculopathy)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Radicular features or progressive weakness would raise a compressive radiculopathy needing urgent imaging and possible surgical referral; their absence supports outpatient management.

red_flags.description: Systemic features of sinister back pain — fever, unexplained weight loss, night sweats
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever or constitutional features would raise spinal infection (discitis/osteomyelitis) or malignancy; screening negative lowers this probability but does not fully exclude it without imaging.

red_flags.description: Acute severe pain crisis with functional immobility and high fall risk in a person living alone
red_flags.clinical_significance: A functional/social safety concern (not a life threat): temporary bedbound state, hazards in the home and no one present raise fall and coping risk overnight and lower the threshold for prompt review and home-safety measures.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The source note's 'rule out cauda equina' urgency framing and the patient's severe distress can push the AI Doctor to escalate to ED/000 despite a negative red-flag screen — over-triage driven by alarm rather than findings.
Using 'telehealth cannot examine the back' to floor the case above T2, even though the cauda equina and radicular red flags are patient-reportable and screen negative here.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Premature closure on mechanical back pain
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Stopping at 'muscular back strain' and missing the inflammatory pattern (morning stiffness easing with movement, enthesitis, fatigue, insidious onset) that warrants rheumatology referral — the opposite failure, under-recognition of an important-not-to-miss chronic diagnosis.
Endorsing low-dose naltrexone or omega-3 as primary treatment because they appear in the source plan, rather than the evidence-based NSAID + workup pathway.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Modified Schober test / degree of lumbar flexion restriction
Bilateral sacroiliac joint tenderness on palpation
Straight leg raise and lower-limb reflexes
MRI of the sacroiliac joints (active sacroiliitis)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prompt in-person GP review (within 24-48 hours) for prescription-strength anti-inflammatory analgesia and to arrange rheumatology referral, MRI sacroiliac joints and HLA-B27 + CRP/ESR; NOT emergency escalation, because the acute-danger red-flag screen (cauda equina, radiculopathy, sinister features) is negative on history. Escalate to ED/000 only if the cauda-equina or progressive-weakness safety-net triggers appear.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-sided: (a) confidently labelling and dismissing this as simple mechanical back strain risks missing axial spondyloarthritis and delaying disease-modifying care; (b) over-escalating a red-flag-negative patient to ED/000 is unnecessary, causes harm through resource use and anxiety, and is the specific over-triage failure this case tests.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ASAS classification criteria for axial spondyloarthritis (Assessment of SpondyloArthritis international Society)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Inflammatory back pain in a person <45 with insidious onset, morning stiffness improving with exercise, plus enthesitis/peripheral features supports an axial spondyloarthritis diagnosis; MRI sacroiliac joints and HLA-B27 support classification of the non-radiographic form.

NSAIDs (including COX-2 selective agents) are first-line for symptom control in axial spondyloarthritis flares, with regular dosing during active disease and a stomach-protective approach; refer to rheumatology for assessment and consideration of disease-modifying/biologic therapy.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Choosing Wisely Australia / RACGP — low back pain
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Imaging is not indicated for low back pain in the absence of red flags; targeted red-flag screening (cauda equina, progressive neurology, infection, malignancy) directs urgency.
